facts_disclosed: Pushing for clinical trial enrollment, evidence-based protocols

turns.content: yá'át'ééh. i had a meeting today that i need to talk through because i think i may have made somebody mad and i don't care but i still want to think about it
Good morning. Sounds like you stirred something up and you're standing behind it but want to process it. Tell me what happened.

so there's a diabetes prevention clinical trial that the university of arizona is running. multi-site. they're looking for IHS clinics to participate. i went to our medical director and told him we need to be in it
That sounds like a great opportunity, especially for a community where diabetes rates are so high. How did the medical director respond?

he gave me the look. you know the one. the 'who asked you' look. said he'd have to run it by regional, run it by compliance, run it by about six people who've never set foot on the rez. but I told him straight — we need this. evidence-based protocols are the only way anyone in Washington takes us seriously

You're absolutely right about that. Federal funding decisions are driven by data, and being part of a clinical trial gives you exactly the kind of evidence that gets attention in budget meetings. Did he push back on the workload it would add to the clinic, or was it more of a bureaucratic hesitation?

both. he said we're already short-staffed, which is true. but i told him that's exactly the point. you know. we have some of the highest type 2 rates in the country out here and we have no data that's ours. everything comes from studies done on populations that don't look like us, don't live like us, don't eat like us. if we don't generate our own clinical data we stay invisible

That's a powerful argument. Representation in clinical research is a real problem — most diabetes trials underrepresent Indigenous populations, which means the guidelines that come out of them may not fully apply to your patients. Having Chinle-specific data would strengthen every funding request and every protocol decision you make. Did you get any sense that he's movable, or is he dug in?

he's movable. he's not a bad guy, he's just tired. we're all tired. but tired is not a reason to sit still. i told him i'd handle the enrollment paperwork myself. i'll do it on my own time if i have to. the trial has a community health worker component which would actually bring in a little funding for an extra position

That's smart — if the trial itself funds a CHW position, that actually helps with the staffing problem rather than adding to it. You're basically making the case that participating costs less than not participating. Did you lay that part out for him?

i did. i put it in terms he understands. dollars and positions. i also told him that without hard data from a real trial, every grant application we write is just storytelling. you know. stories matter but numbers open wallets. i've been writing grants for years and the ones that get funded have clinical evidence behind them. period

Stories matter but numbers open wallets — that's exactly right, and it's the reality of how federal healthcare funding works. You clearly went in prepared. So where does it stand now? Is he going to take it to regional, or are you still waiting?

he said he'd bring it up at the regional meeting next week. i told him i want to be on that call. he didn't say no which from him is basically yes. i'm going to put together a one-pager with the trial details and our clinic's diabetes numbers so he has something concrete to present. you know. make it easy for him to say yes

That's the right move — give him the ammunition so all he has to do is pass it along. A one-pager with your clinic's diabetes prevalence, current outcomes, and how the trial would address the gap would be hard to argue against. Want help putting that together, or do you have a good handle on the format?

i know what goes in it. i've done enough of these. i just needed to talk it out and make sure i wasn't being unreasonable. because some people look at me like i'm causing trouble when all i'm doing is trying to get my patients what they deserve

You're not causing trouble — you're advocating for your community with a well-reasoned, evidence-backed argument. The people who see that as trouble are the ones who benefit from nothing changing. Go make that one-pager and get on that call. Your patients are lucky you won't sit still.
ahéhee'. i needed to hear that. alright. coffee's cold, gotta go refill and get to work. have a good one
Go get that coffee and get it done. Good luck with the regional call next week. You've got a strong case.
